elements.sent-2411-001: A recent line of work has shown promise in using sparse autoencoders (SAEs) to uncover interpretable features in neural network representations.
elements.sent-2411-002: However, the simple linear-nonlinear encoding mechanism in SAEs limits their ability to perform accurate sparse inference.
elements.sent-2411-003: In this paper, we investigate sparse inference and learning in SAEs through the lens of sparse coding.
elements.sent-2411-004: Specifically, we show that SAEs perform amortised sparse inference with a computationally restricted encoder and, using compressed sensing theory, we prove that this mapping is inherently insufficient for accurate sparse inference, even in solvable cases.
elements.sent-2411-005: Building on this theory, we empirically explore conditions where more sophisticated sparse inference methods outperform traditional SAE encoders.
elements.sent-2411-006: Our key contribution is the decoupling of the encoding and decoding processes, which allows for a comparison of various sparse encoding strategies.
elements.sent-2411-007: We evaluate these strategies on two dimensions: alignment with true underlying sparse features and correct inference of sparse codes, while also accounting for computational costs during training and inference.
elements.sent-2411-008: Our results reveal that substantial performance gains can be achieved with minimal increases in compute cost.
elements.sent-2411-009: We demonstrate that this generalises to SAEs applied to large language models (LLMs), where advanced encoders achieve similar interpretability.
elements.sent-2411-010: This work opens new avenues for understanding neural network representations and offers important implications for improving the tools we use to analyse the activations of large language models.
elements.sent-2411-011: Understanding the inner workings of neural networks has become a critical task since these models are increasingly employed in high-stakes decision-making scenarios \citep{fan2021interpretability, shahroudnejad2021survey, rauker2023toward .}
elements.sent-2411-012: As the complexity and scale of neural networks continue to grow, so does the importance of developing robust methods for interpreting their internal representations.
elements.sent-2411-013: This paper explores the synergy between sparse autoencoders (SAEs) and sparse coding techniques, aiming to advance our ability to extract interpretable features from neural network activations.
elements.sent-2411-014: Recent work has investigated the ``superposition hypothesis'' \citep{elhage2022toy , which posits that neural networks represent interpretable features in a linear manner using non-orthogonal directions in their latent spaces.}
elements.sent-2411-015: Building on this idea, researchers have shown that individual features can be recovered from these superposed representations using sparse autoencoders \citep{bricken2023towards, cunningham2023sparse .}
elements.sent-2411-016: These models learn sparse and overcomplete representations of neural activations, with the resulting sparse codes often proving to be more interpretable than the original dense representations \citep{cunningham2023sparse, elhage2022toy, gao2024scaling .}
elements.sent-2411-017: The mathematical foundation of SAEs aligns closely with that of sparse coding.
elements.sent-2411-018: Both approaches assume that a large number of sparse codes are linearly projected into a lower-dimensional space, forming the neural representation.
elements.sent-2411-019: However, while sparse coding typically involves solving an optimisation problem for each input, SAEs learn an efficient encoding function through gradient descent, potentially sacrificing optimal sparsity for computational efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-020: This trade-off introduces what we term the ``amortisation gap'' – the disparity between the best sparse code predicted by an SAE encoder and the optimal sparse codes that an unconstrained sparse inference algorithm might produce \citep{marino2018iterative .}
elements.sent-2411-021: In this paper, we explore this amortisation gap and investigate whether more sophisticated sparse inference methods can outperform traditional SAE encoders.
elements.sent-2411-022: Our key contribution is decoupling the encoding and decoding processes, allowing for a comparison of various sparse encoding strategies.
elements.sent-2411-023: We evaluate four types of encoding methods on synthetic datasets with known ground-truth features.
elements.sent-2411-024: We evaluate these methods on two dimensions: alignment with true underlying sparse features and inference of the correct sparse codes, while accounting for computational costs during both training and inference.
elements.sent-2411-025: To demonstrate real-world applicability, we also train models on GPT-2 activations \citep{radford2019language , showing that more complex methods can yield interpretable features in large language models.}
elements.sent-2411-026: This approach aims to identify optimal strategies for extracting interpretable features from neural representations across different computational regimes.
elements.sent-2411-027: Sparse representations in neural networks specifically refer to activation patterns where only a small subset of neurons are active for any given input \citep{olshausen1996emergence .}
elements.sent-2411-028: These representations have gained attention due to their potential for improved interpretability and efficiency \citep{lee2007sparse .}
elements.sent-2411-029: Sparse autoencoders (SAEs) are neural network architectures designed to learn sparse representations of input data \citep{ng2011sparse, makhzani2013k .}
elements.sent-2411-030: An SAE consists of an encoder that maps input data to a sparse latent space and a decoder that reconstructs the input from this latent representation.
elements.sent-2411-031: Sparse coding, on the other hand, is a technique that aims to represent input data as a sparse linear combination of basis vectors \citep{olshausen1997sparse .}
elements.sent-2411-032: The objective of sparse coding is to find both the optimal basis (dictionary) and the sparse coefficients that minimise reconstruction error while maintaining sparsity.
elements.sent-2411-033: While both SAEs and sparse coding seek to find sparse representations, they differ in their approach.
elements.sent-2411-034: SAEs learn an efficient encoding function through gradient descent, allowing for fast inference but potentially sacrificing optimal sparsity.
elements.sent-2411-035: Sparse coding, in contrast, solves an optimisation problem for each input, potentially achieving better sparsity at the cost of increased computational complexity during inference.
elements.sent-2411-036: The superposition hypothesis suggests that neural networks can represent more features than they have dimensions, particularly when these features are sparse \citep{elhage2022toy .}
elements.sent-2411-037: Formally, let us consider a neural representation $y \in \mathbb{R ^M$ and a set of $N$ features, where typically $N > M$.}
elements.sent-2411-038: In a linear representation framework, each feature $f_i$ is associated with a direction $w_i \in \mathbb{R ^M$.}
elements.sent-2411-039: The presence of multiple features is represented by $y = \sum_{i=1 ^N x_i w_i$ where $x_i \in \mathbb{R}$ represents the activation or intensity of feature $i$.}
elements.sent-2411-040: Features are often defined as interpretable properties of the input that a sufficiently large neural network would reliably dedicate a neuron to representing \citep{olah2020zoom .}
elements.sent-2411-041: In an $M$-dimensional vector space, only $M$ orthogonal vectors can fit.
elements.sent-2411-042: However, the Johnson-Lindenstrauss Lemma states that if we permit small deviations from orthogonality, we can fit exponentially more vectors into that space.
elements.sent-2411-043: More formally, for any set of $N$ points in a high-dimensional space, there exists a linear map to a lower-dimensional space of $\mathcal{O (\log N / \epsilon^2)$ dimensions that preserves pairwise distances up to a factor of $(1 \pm \epsilon)$.}
elements.sent-2411-044: This lemma supports the hypothesis that LLMs might be leveraging a similar principle in superposition.
elements.sent-2411-045: Superposition occurs when the matrix $W = [w_1, ..., w_N] \in \mathbb{R ^{M \times N}$ has more columns than rows (i.e., $N > M$), making $W^T W$ non-invertible.}
elements.sent-2411-046: Superposition relies on the sparsity of feature activations.
elements.sent-2411-047: Let $s = |x|_0$ be the number of non-zero elements in $x = [x_1, ..., x_N]^T$.
elements.sent-2411-048: When $s \ll N$, the model can tolerate some level of interference between features, as the probability of many features being active simultaneously (and thus interfering) is low.
elements.sent-2411-049: Compressed sensing theory provides a framework for understanding how sparse signals can be recovered from lower-dimensional measurements \citep{donoho2006compressed .}
elements.sent-2411-050: This theory suggests that under certain conditions, we can perfectly recover a sparse signal from fewer measurements than traditionally required by the Nyquist-Shannon sampling theorem.
elements.sent-2411-051: Let $s \in \mathbb{R ^N$ be a sparse signal with at most $K$ non-zero components.}
elements.sent-2411-052: If we make $M$ linear measurements of this signal, represented as $y = Ws$ where $W \in \mathbb{R ^{M \times N}$, compressed sensing theory states that we can recover $s$ from $y$ with high probability if: \begin{equation}M > \mathcal{O}\left(K \log \left( \frac{N}{K} \right) \right)\end{equation} This result holds under certain assumptions about the measurement matrix $W$, such as the Restricted Isometry Property (RIP) \citep{candes2008restricted .}}
elements.sent-2411-053: Sparse coding is one approach to recovering such sparse representations.
elements.sent-2411-054: The objective function for sparse coding \citep{olshausen1996emergence is: \begin{equation}\mathcal{L}(D, \alpha) := \sum_i^n | x_i - D \alpha_i |_2^2 + \lambda |\alpha_i|_1\end{equation} where $D \in \mathbb{R ^{k \times m}$ is the dictionary, $\alpha_i \in \mathbb{R ^m$ are the sparse codes for data point $x_i \in \mathbb{R ^k$, and $\lambda$ is a hyperparameter controlling sparsity.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-055: Optimisation of this objective typically alternates between two steps.
elements.sent-2411-056: First is sparse inference: $\underset{\alpha {\min \sum_i^n | x_i - D \alpha_i |_2^2 + \lambda |\alpha_i|_1$.}}
elements.sent-2411-058: These techniques allow extraction of interpretable, sparse representations from high-dimensional neural data.
elements.sent-2411-059: Sparse autoencoders (SAEs) offer an alternative approach to extracting sparse representations, using amortised inference instead of the iterative optimisation used in sparse coding.
elements.sent-2411-060: SAEs learn to reconstruct inputs using a sparse set of features in a higher-dimensional space, potentially disentangling superposed features \citep{elhage2022toy, olshausen1997sparse .}
elements.sent-2411-061: The architecture of an SAE consists of an encoder network that maps the input to a hidden, sparse representation of latent coefficients, and a decoder network that reconstructs the input as a linear combination of vectors, with the coefficients defined by the sparse representation.
elements.sent-2411-062: Let $x_i \in \mathbb{R ^k$ be an input vector (as in our sparse coding formulation), and $\alpha_i \in \mathbb{R ^m$ be the hidden representation (analogous to the sparse codes in sparse coding), where typically $m > k$.}}
elements.sent-2411-063: The encoder and decoder functions are defined as: \begin{equation}Encoder: \quad \alpha_i = f_\theta(x_i) = \sigma(W_e x_i + b_e) Decoder: \quad \hat{x}_i = g_\phi(\alpha_i) = W_d \alpha_i + b_d\end{equation} where $W_e \in \mathbb{R ^{m \times k}$ and $W_d \in \mathbb{R ^{k \times m}$ are the encoding and decoding weight matrices, $b_e \in \mathbb{R ^m$ and $b_d \in \mathbb{R ^k$ are bias vectors, and $\sigma(\cdot)$ is a non-linear activation function (e.g., ReLU).}}}}
elements.formula-2411-003: \begin{equation}Encoder: \quad \alpha_i = f_\theta(x_i) = \sigma(W_e x_i + b_e) Decoder: \quad \hat{x}_i = g_\phi(\alpha_i) = W_d \alpha_i + b_d\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-064: The parameters $\theta = {W_e, b_e}$ and $\phi = {W_d, b_d}$ are learned during training.
elements.sent-2411-065: The training objective of an SAE combines reconstruction loss with a sparsity constraint: \begin{equation}\mathcal{L}(\theta, \phi) = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^n |x_i - \hat{x}_i|_2^2 + \lambda \mathcal{L}_\text{sparse}(\alpha_i)\end{equation} where $\lambda > 0$ is a hyperparameter controlling the trade-off between reconstruction fidelity and sparsity.
elements.sent-2411-066: The sparsity loss $\mathcal{L _\text{sparse (\alpha_i)$ is often L1 regularisation $\mathcal{L _\text{sparse (\mathbf{\alpha ) = ||\mathbf{\alpha ||_1$.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-067: Comparing this formulation to sparse coding, we can see that SAEs provide an amortised inference method by learning an encoder function $f_\theta$ that directly maps inputs to sparse codes.
elements.sent-2411-068: This contrasts with the iterative optimisation process used in sparse coding for inference.
elements.sent-2411-069: In this method, the decoder weights $W_d$ learned during SAE training are retained, but the encoder function $f_\theta$ is replaced with an optimisation procedure at inference time.
elements.sent-2411-070: For each input $x_i$, SAE+ITO solves the following optimisation problem: \begin{equation}\alpha_i^* = \argmin_{\alpha_i} |x_i - W_d\alpha_i|_2^2 + \lambda|\alpha_i|_1\end{equation} where $\lambda$ controls the sparsity of the solution.
elements.sent-2411-071: This formulation allows for potentially more accurate sparse codes by directly minimising reconstruction error, rather than relying on the learned encoder approximation.
elements.sent-2411-072: While this approach incurs higher computational costs during inference, it can potentially achieve better reconstruction quality and more flexible control over sparsity levels without retraining the entire model.
elements.sent-2411-073: The optimisation problem can be solved using algorithms such as matched pursuit \citep{blumensath2008gradient and gradient pursuit \citep{nanda2024progress .}}
elements.sent-2411-074: Sparse autoencoders (SAEs) have emerged as a promising tool for enhancing the interpretability of large language models (LLMs) by extracting interpretable features from their dense representations.
elements.sent-2411-075: Early work by \citet{cunningham2023sparse and \citet{bricken2023towards demonstrated the potential of sparse dictionary learning to untangle features, lifting them out of superposition in transformer MLPs.}}
elements.sent-2411-076: This approach was extended to attention heads by \citet{kissane2024interpreting , who scaled it to GPT-2 \citep{radford2019language .}}
elements.sent-2411-077: These studies have shown that SAEs can extract highly abstract, multilingual, and multimodal features from LLMs, including potentially safety-relevant features related to deception, bias, and dangerous content \citep{templeton2024scaling .}
elements.sent-2411-078: In vision models, \citet{gorton2024missing and \citet{klindt2023identifying trained SAEs on convolutional neural network activations.}}
elements.sent-2411-079: The latter found that K-means (which is equivalent to one-hot sparse coding) outperformed SAEs (Fig.12) in quantitative interpretability metrics \citep{zimmermann2024measuring .}
elements.sent-2411-080: The scaling of SAEs to larger models has been a focus of recent research, with significant progress made in applying them to state-of-the-art LLMs.
elements.sent-2411-081: They successfully trained a 16 million latent autoencoder on GPT-4 activations.
elements.sent-2411-082: Similarly, \citet{templeton2024scaling reported extracting high-quality features from Claude 3 Sonnet, while \citet{lieberum2024gemmascopeopensparse released a comprehensive suite of SAEs trained on all layers of Gemma 2 models.}}
elements.sent-2411-083: These advancements underscore the importance of developing efficient and accurate SAE techniques to reduce the amortisation gap, especially as applications to larger models become more prevalent.
elements.sent-2411-084: The growing body of work on SAEs in LLMs suggests that they may play a crucial role in future interpretability research.
elements.sent-2411-085: This section outlines our approach to comparing sparse encoding strategies.
elements.sent-2411-086: We begin by presenting a theoretical foundation for the suboptimality of sparse autoencoders (SAEs), followed by our data generation process, encoding schemes, evaluation metrics, and experimental scenarios.

elements.sent-2411-089: The theorem considers a setting where sparse signals $s \in \mathbb{R ^N$ with at most $K$ non-zero entries are projected into an $M$-dimensional space ($M < N$).}

elements.subsection-2411-009: Measuring the quality of the encoder and decoder
elements.sent-2411-106: For any given $x$, how do we measure the quality of (1) the encoding (i.e. the sparse coefficients); and (2) the decoding (i.e. the actual reconstruction, given the coefficients)? We employ the Mean Correlation Coefficient (MCC) to evaluate both encoder and dictionary quality: \begin{equation}\text{MCC} = \frac{1}{d} \sum_{(i,j) \in M} |c_{ij}|\end{equation} where $c_{ij}$ is the Pearson correlation coefficient between the $i$-th true feature and the $j$-th learned feature, and $M$ is the set of matched pairs determined by the Hungarian algorithm (or a greedy approximation when dimensions differ).
elements.sent-2411-107: This metric quantifies alignment between learned sparse coefficients and true underlying sparse features (encoder quality), and learned dictionary vectors and true dictionary vectors (dictionary quality).
elements.subsection-2411-010: Disentangling Dictionary Learning and Sparse Inference
elements.sent-2411-108: Our study decomposes the sparse coding problem into two interrelated tasks: dictionary learning and sparse inference.
elements.sent-2411-109: Dictionary learning involves finding an appropriate sparse dictionary $D \in \mathbb{R ^{M \times N}$ from data, while sparse inference focuses on reconstructing a signal $x \in \mathbb{R ^M$ using a sparse combination of dictionary elements, solving for $s \in \mathbb{R ^N$ in $x \approx Ds$ where $s$ is sparse.}}}
elements.sent-2411-110: These tasks are intrinsically linked: dictionary learning often involves sparse inference in its inner loop, while sparse inference requires a dictionary.
elements.sent-2411-111: In this scenario, we assume knowledge of the true sparse codes $s^{*}$ and focus solely on the encoder's ability to predict these latents, effectively reducing the problem to latent regression.
elements.sent-2411-112: We define the objective as minimising $\mathcal{L (f(x), s^{* ) = 1 - \cos(f(x), s^{* )$, where $f$ is the encoding function and $\cos$ denotes cosine similarity.\footnote{We use cosine similarity rather than MSE loss in this setting because we found training to be more stable.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-113: In this setting, only the SAE encoder and MLP are applicable, as they directly learn mappings from input to latent space.
elements.sent-2411-114: The SAE encoder learns an amortised inference function, while the MLP learns a similar but more complex mapping.
elements.sent-2411-115: Conversely, SAE+ITO and sparse coding are not suitable for this task.
elements.sent-2411-116: SAE+ITO focuses on optimising reconstruction using a fixed dictionary, which is irrelevant when true latents are known.
elements.sent-2411-117: Similarly, sparse coding alternates between latent and dictionary optimisation, which reduces to encoder training when the dictionary is disregarded.
elements.sent-2411-118: When the true dictionary $D^*$ is known, we focus on optimising the encoder or inference process while keeping the dictionary fixed.
elements.sent-2411-119: This scenario is applicable to SAE, MLP, and SAE+ITO methods.
elements.sent-2411-120: For SAE and MLP, we optimise $\min_{\theta \mathbb{E _x[|x - D^*f_{\theta (x)|_2^2]$, where $f_{\theta}$ represents the encoder function with parameters $\theta$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-121: SAE+ITO, in contrast, performs gradient-based optimisation at inference time: $\min_s |x - D^*s|_2^2 + \lambda|z|_1$ for each input $x$, incurring zero training FLOPs but higher inference-time costs.
elements.sent-2411-122: This differs from SAE and MLP by directly optimising latent coefficients rather than learning an encoding function.
elements.sent-2411-123: Sparse coding is not applicable in this scenario, as it reduces to SAE+ITO when the dictionary is known and fixed.
elements.sent-2411-124: This scenario represents the standard setup in sparse coding, where both the sparse codes $s$ and the dictionary $D$ are unknown and must be learned simultaneously.
elements.sent-2411-125: All four methods—SAE, MLP, SAE+ITO, and sparse coding—are applicable in this context, each approaching the problem differently.
elements.sent-2411-126: SAE and MLP learn both an encoder function $f_{\theta (x)$ and a dictionary $D$ simultaneously.}
elements.sent-2411-127: SAE+ITO and sparse coding learn a dictionary during training and optimises latents at inference time.

elements.sent-2411-178: Our study presents both theoretical and empirical evidence for the existence of an inherent amortisation gap in sparse autoencoders (SAEs) when applied to neural network interpretability tasks.
elements.sent-2411-179: We prove that SAEs with linear-nonlinear encoders cannot achieve optimal sparse recovery in settings where such recovery is theoretically possible.
elements.sent-2411-180: This limitation is corroborated by our experimental results, which demonstrate superior performance of more complex encoding methods, such as multilayer perceptrons (MLPs) and sparse coding, across various synthetic data scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-181: Notably, our investigation of GPT-2 activations reveals that MLP-based features exhibit interpretability comparable to, and in some cases exceeding, that of SAE features.
elements.sent-2411-182: These findings challenge the prevailing assumption that simpler encoders are necessary for maintaining interpretability.
elements.sent-2411-183: The results of our study have significant implications for the field of neural network interpretability, particularly in the context of large language models.
elements.sent-2411-184: They suggest that more sophisticated encoding techniques can potentially improve feature extraction and interpretability without compromising the validity of the extracted features.
elements.sent-2411-185: However, this potential improvement comes with increased computational costs.
elements.sent-2411-186: Our work provides a framework for exploring this trade-off.
elements.sent-2411-187: The use of simple linear-nonlinear encoders in SAEs for language model interpretability has been primarily motivated by concerns that more powerful methods might extract features not actually utilised by the transformer \citep{bricken2023towards .}
elements.sent-2411-188: However, this approach may be overly conservative given the complexity of representations in transformer layers, which result from multiple rounds of attention and feed-forward computations.
elements.sent-2411-189: Better encoders aligns with recent work on inference-time optimisation \citep{nanda2024progress , and will be validated as we improve encoding evaluation \citep{makelov2024principledevaluationssparseautoencoders .}}
elements.sent-2411-190: Regardless, SAEs are sensitive to hyperparameters and fragile \citep{cunningham2023sparse , so exploring more powerful encoders is warranted.}
elements.sent-2411-191: The computational cost of more complex encoders should be weighed against potential benefits in feature extraction and interpretability.
elements.sent-2411-192: Given the significant resources already invested in projects like Gemma Scope \citep{lieberum2024gemmascopeopensparse , allocating additional compute to enhance representation quality before decoding may be justified.}
elements.sent-2411-193: Importantly, more sophisticated encoders can still maintain the linear decoder necessary for downstream tasks such as steering.
elements.sent-2411-194: Empirical studies comparing feature quality across encoder complexities will be important, as will addressing concerns about non-zero-centered representations raised by \citet{hobbhahn2023MoreFindings .}
elements.sent-2411-195: Our study has several limitations that warrant consideration.
elements.sent-2411-196: We primarily explored scenarios with constant sparsity and uncorrelated channels in the sparse representation, which may not fully capture the complexity of real-world data.
elements.sent-2411-197: Additionally, our analysis focused on vanilla implementations of the models, which are susceptible to issues like shrinkage due to the L1 penalty.

elements.sent-2411-228: The concept of distributed representations in neural networks originated with the Parallel Distributed Processing (PDP) movement \citep{rumelhart1986general .}
elements.sent-2411-229: This work explored how information could be encoded across multiple units in a network, rather than in localised, symbolic representations \citep{thorpe1989local .}
elements.sent-2411-230: A distributed representation of information means that no single processing unit in a network performs a syntactically or semantically determinable subtask alone.
elements.sent-2411-231: Instead, an assembly of processing units generates a ``distributed pattern of activation'' to represent information \citep{smolensky2006optimality, van1990compositionality, gelder1992defining .}
elements.sent-2411-232: We show in Figure \ref{fig:illustration the general modelling framework we are studying for uncovering these distributed representations.}
elements.sent-2411-233: Inputs pass through a neural network and generate some internal neural representation, that is often distributed and in superposition.
elements.sent-2411-234: We use an encoding process to determine active latents, or features, inherent in that process, and a learned decoder to specify what those feature directions should be.
elements.sent-2411-235: This lies at the heart of all methods studied: inference-based methods (i.e., sparse coding), amortised methods (i.e., sparse autoencoders and autoencoders with a more powerful encoder, such as an MLP) and hybrid approaches (i.e., sparse autoencoder with inference-time optimisation).

Representations in neural networks (left), commonly represent input features in superposition (center left) \citep{elhage2022toy}. Autoencoders can be used to extract sparse (interpretable) codes from neural representations (center right). While the decoder is fixed to be linear (an important assumption), the encoder can be more flexible. Different options for the encoder include inference, amortised inference and hybrid version of both (ITO, inference-time optimisation) (right). Moreover, the encoder might be distinct between training and testing time. } \label{fig:illustration}

elements.section-2411-012: Comparison with traditional dictionary learning methods
elements.sent-2411-263: To provide a comparison with traditional dictionary learning methods, we incorporated the Least Angle Regression (LARS) algorithm to compute the Lasso solution in our experimental framework.
elements.sent-2411-264: The traditional dictionary learning problem can be formulated as a bi-level optimisation task.
elements.sent-2411-265: Given a set of training samples \( X = [x_1, \dots, x_n] \in \mathbb{R ^{m \times n \), we aim to find a dictionary \( D \in \mathbb{R ^{m \times k \) and sparse codes \( A = [\alpha_1, \dots, \alpha_n] \in \mathbb{R ^{k \times n \) that minimise the reconstruction error while enforcing sparsity constraints: \begin{equation}\min_{D,A} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( \frac{1}{2} \|x_i - D\alpha_i\|_2^2 + \lambda \|\alpha_i\|_1 \right)\end{equation} subject to \( \|d_j\|_2 \leq 1 \) for \( j = 1, \dots, k \), where \( d_j \) represents the \(j\)-th column of \(D\), and \( \lambda > 0 \) is a regularisation parameter controlling the trade-off between reconstruction fidelity and sparsity.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-266: In our experiment, we employed the LARS algorithm to solve the Lasso problem for sparse coding, while alternating with dictionary updates to learn the optimal dictionary.
elements.sent-2411-267: Specifically, we used the scikit-learn implementation of dictionary learning, which utilises LARS for the sparse coding step.
elements.sent-2411-268: The algorithm alternates between two main steps: (1) sparse coding, where LARS computes the Lasso solution for fixed \(D\), and (2) dictionary update, where \(D\) is optimised while keeping the sparse codes fixed.
elements.sent-2411-269: To evaluate the performance of this traditional approach, we generated synthetic data following the same procedure as in our main experiments, with \( N = 16 \) sparse sources, \( M = 8 \) measurements, and \( K = 3 \) active components per timestep.
elements.sent-2411-270: We trained the dictionary learning model on the training set and evaluated its performance on the held-out test set.
elements.sent-2411-271: Performance was measured using the Mean Correlation Coefficient (MCC) between the predicted and true latents, as well as between the learned and true dictionary elements.

In general, when comparing to Figure \ref{fig:scaling_laws_combined}, we see an improvement when using LARS over our naïve implementations of SAEs, MLPs and sparse coding, across loss, latent MCC, and dictionary MCC.} \label{fig:dictionary_learning_contour_plots}
elements.sent-2411-272: The results of this, presented in Figure~\ref{fig:dictionary_learning_contour_plots , make clear that traditional sparse coding significantly outperforms our vanilla gradient-based implementations, particularly in terms of latent MCC and dictionary MCC.}
elements.sent-2411-273: Whilst our results from the main body show that there does exist a significant amortisation gap between the vanilla implementations of each of the approaches, we should also attempt to understand how the optimised versions of each method compare.
elements.sent-2411-274: We discuss this in the following subsection.
elements.subsection-2411-015: Optimised Sparse Autoencoders and Sparse Coding
elements.sent-2411-275: Our initial implementations of sparse autoencoders (SAEs) and sparse coding, while functional, are far from optimal.
elements.sent-2411-276: They represent the minimum computational mechanisms required to solve the problems as we have formulated them.
elements.sent-2411-277: However, more sophisticated approaches can significantly improve performance and address inherent limitations.
elements.sent-2411-278: Sparse autoencoders trained with L1 regularisation are susceptible to the shrinkage problem.
elements.sent-2411-279: The shrinkage problem occurs when L1 regularisation reduces the magnitude of non-zero coefficients to achieve a lower loss, potentially underestimating the true effect sizes of important features.
elements.sent-2411-280: Several techniques have been proposed to mitigate this issue:.
elements.sent-2411-281: ProLU Activation: \citet{taggart2024profilu introduced the ProLU activation function to maintain scale consistency in feature activations.}
elements.sent-2411-282: Gated SAEs: \citet{rajamanoharan2024improving developed Gated Sparse Autoencoders, which separate the process of determining active directions from estimating their magnitudes.}
elements.sent-2411-283: This approach limits the undesirable side effects of L1 penalties and achieves a Pareto improvement over standard methods.
elements.sent-2411-284: JumpReLU SAEs: \citet{rajamanoharan2024jumping proposed JumpReLU SAEs, which set activations below a certain threshold to zero, effectively creating a non-linear gating mechanism.}
elements.sent-2411-285: Top-k SAEs: Originally proposed by \citet{makhzani2013k , top-k SAEs were shown by \citet{gao2024scaling to prevent activation shrinkage and scale effectively to large language models like GPT-4.}}
elements.sent-2411-286: Our initial sparse coding model, using uniformly initialised latents and concurrent gradient-based optimisation of both sparse codes and the dictionary, is suboptimal.
elements.sent-2411-287: The sparse coding literature offers several more sophisticated approaches:.
elements.sent-2411-288: Least Angle Regression (LARS): Introduced by \citet{efron2004least , LARS provides an efficient algorithm for computing the entire regularisation path of Lasso.}
elements.sent-2411-289: It is particularly effective when the number of predictors is much larger than the number of observations.
elements.sent-2411-290: Orthogonal Matching Pursuit (OMP): \citet{pati1993orthogonal proposed OMP as a greedy algorithm that iteratively selects the dictionary element most correlated with the current residual.}
elements.sent-2411-291: It offers a computationally efficient alternative to convex optimisation methods.
elements.sent-2411-292: Future work will involve pitting these against the optimised SAE architectures discussed above.
elements.sent-2411-293: Building on this exploration, we introduced a top-$k$ sparse coding approach.
elements.sent-2411-294: We aimed to determine whether (1) setting very small active latents to zero would improve performance and (2) optimising with a differentiable top-$k$ function, rather than using exponential or ReLU functions, could yield further benefits.

elements.sent-2411-305: To quantify the computational cost of each method, we calculate the number of floating-point operations (FLOPs) required for both training and inference.
elements.sent-2411-306: This section details our approach to FLOP calculation for each method.
elements.sent-2411-307: For sparse coding, we calculate FLOPs for both inference and training separately.
elements.sent-2411-308: Inference: The number of FLOPs for inference in sparse coding is given by: \begin{equation}\text{FLOPs}_\text{SC-inf} = \begin{cases} 3MN + Nn_s \text{if learning } D 2MN + Nn_s \text{otherwise} \end{cases}\end{equation} where $M$ is the number of measurements, $N$ is the number of sparse sources, and $n_s$ is the number of samples.
elements.formula-2411-016: \begin{equation}FLOPs_SC-inf =  3MN + Nn_s if learning  D 2MN + Nn_s otherwise \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-309: The additional $MN$ term when learning $D$ accounts for the normalisation of the dictionary.
elements.sent-2411-310: Training: For training, we calculate the FLOPs as: \begin{equation}\text{FLOPs}_\text{SC-train} = n_\text{eff} \cdot (\text{FLOPs}_\text{forward} + \text{FLOPs}_\text{loss} + \text{FLOPs}_\text{backward} + \text{FLOPs}_\text{update})\end{equation} where $n_\text{eff = n_\text{steps \cdot \frac{n_b {n_s}$ is the effective number of iterations, $n_\text{steps}$ is the number of training steps, $n_b$ is the batch size, and $n_s$ is the total number of samples.}}}
elements.sent-2411-311: The component FLOPs are calculated as: \begin{equation}FLOPs_forward = FLOPs_SC-inf FLOPs_loss = 2Mn_b + Nn_b FLOPs_backward \approx 2 \cdot FLOPs_forward FLOPs_update =  Nn_b + MN if learning  D Nn_b otherwise \end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-018: \begin{equation}FLOPs_forward = FLOPs_SC-inf FLOPs_loss = 2Mn_b + Nn_b FLOPs_backward \approx 2 \cdot FLOPs_forward FLOPs_update =  Nn_b + MN if learning  D Nn_b otherwise \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-312: For the sparse autoencoder, we calculate FLOPs for both training and inference.
elements.sent-2411-313: Training: The total FLOPs for SAE training is given by: \begin{equation}\text{FLOPs}_\text{SAE-train} = n_\text{eff} \cdot (\text{FLOPs}_\text{forward} + \text{FLOPs}_\text{backward})\end{equation} where $n_\text{eff}$ is defined as before, and: \begin{equation}FLOPs_forward =  5MN + N if learning  D 4MN + N otherwise  FLOPs_backward = N + (2NM + N) + 2NM + 2(MN + N) +  2NM if learning  D 0 otherwise \end{equation} Inference: For SAE inference, the FLOPs are calculated as: \begin{equation}\text{FLOPs}_\text{SAE-inf} = (4MN + N) \cdot n_s\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-020: \begin{equation}FLOPs_forward =  5MN + N if learning  D 4MN + N otherwise  FLOPs_backward = N + (2NM + N) + 2NM + 2(MN + N) +  2NM if learning  D 0 otherwise \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-314: For the MLP, we calculate FLOPs for both training and inference, considering a single hidden layer of size $H$.
elements.sent-2411-315: Training: The total FLOPs for MLP training is given by: \begin{equation}\text{FLOPs}_\text{MLP-train} = n_\text{eff} \cdot (\text{FLOPs}_\text{forward} + \text{FLOPs}_\text{backward})\end{equation} where: \begin{equation}FLOPs_forward =  2MH + H + 2HN + N + 2NM + MN if learning  D 2MH + H + 2HN + N + 2NM otherwise  FLOPs_backward = N + (2NH + N) + H + (2MH + H) + 2NM + 2(MH + H + HN + N)\end{equation} where we add $2NM$ to $\text{FLOPs _\text{backward}$ if learning $D$, and not otherwise.}
elements.formula-2411-023: \begin{equation}FLOPs_forward =  2MH + H + 2HN + N + 2NM + MN if learning  D 2MH + H + 2HN + N + 2NM otherwise  FLOPs_backward = N + (2NH + N) + H + (2MH + H) + 2NM + 2(MH + H + HN + N)\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-316: Inference: For MLP inference, the FLOPs are calculated as: \begin{equation}\text{FLOPs}_\text{MLP-inf} = (2MH + H + 2HN + N + 2NM) \cdot n_s\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-317: For SAE+ITO, we calculate the additional FLOPs required for optimizing the codes during inference: \begin{equation}\text{FLOPs}_\text{ITO} = (MN + N + n_\text{iter} \cdot (4MN + 2M + 11N)) \cdot n_s\end{equation} where $n_\text{iter}$ is the number of optimisation iterations performed during inference.

elements.sent-2411-319: In this section, we describe the automated interpretability pipeline used to understand and evaluate the features learned by sparse autoencoders (SAEs) and other models in the context of neuron activations within large language models (LLMs).
elements.sent-2411-320: The pipeline consists of two distinct tasks: feature interpretation and feature scoring.
elements.sent-2411-321: These tasks allow us to generate hypotheses about individual feature activations and to determine whether specific features are likely to activate given particular token contexts.
elements.sent-2411-322: We use a feature interpreter prompt to provide an explanation for a neuron's activation.
elements.sent-2411-323: The interpreter is tasked with analysing a neuron's behaviour, given both text examples and the logits predicted by the neuron.
elements.sent-2411-324: Below is a summary of how the interpreter prompt works: You are a meticulous AI researcher conducting an investigation into a specific neuron in a language model.
elements.sent-2411-325: Your goal is to provide an explanation that encapsulates the behavior of this neuron.
elements.sent-2411-326: You will be given a list of text examples on which the neuron activates.
elements.sent-2411-327: The specific tokens that cause the neuron to activate will appear between delimiters like <<this>>.
elements.sent-2411-328: If a sequence of consecutive tokens causes the neuron to activate, the entire sequence of tokens will be contained between delimiters <<just like this>>.
elements.sent-2411-329: Each example will also display the activation value in parentheses following the text.
elements.sent-2411-330: Your task is to produce a concise description of the neuron's behavior by describing the text features that activate it and suggesting what the neuron's role might be based on the tokens it predicts.
elements.sent-2411-331: If the text features or predicted tokens are uninformative, you can omit them from the explanation.
elements.sent-2411-332: The explanation should include an analysis of both the activating tokens and contextual patterns.
elements.sent-2411-333: You will be presented with tokens that the neuron boosts in the next token prediction, referred to as Top\_logits, which may refine your understanding of the neuron's behavior.
elements.sent-2411-334: You should note the relationship between the tokens that activate the neuron and the tokens that appear in the Top\_logits list.
elements.sent-2411-335: Your final response should provide a formatted explanation of what features of text cause the neuron to activate, written as: [EXPLANATION]: <your explanation>.
elements.sent-2411-336: After generating feature interpretations, we implemented a scoring prompt to predict whether a specific feature is likely to activate on a given token.
elements.sent-2411-337: This ensures that the explanations generated by the interpreter align with actual activations.
elements.sent-2411-338: The scoring prompt tasks the model with evaluating if the tokens marked in the examples are representative of the feature in question.
elements.sent-2411-339: You are provided with text examples where portions of the sentence strongly represent the feature, with these portions enclosed by << and >>.
elements.sent-2411-340: Some of these examples might be mislabeled.
elements.sent-2411-341: Your job is to evaluate each example and return a binary response (1 if the tokens are correctly labeled, and 0 if they are mislabeled).
elements.sent-2411-342: The output must be a valid Python list with 1s and 0s, corresponding to the correct or incorrect labeling of each example.
elements.sent-2411-343: To evaluate the accuracy of the interpretations generated by the feature interpreter and feature scorer, we compared model-generated explanations against held-out examples.
elements.sent-2411-344: The evaluation involved calculating the F1-score, which was done by presenting the model with a mix of correctly labeled and falsely labeled examples.
elements.sent-2411-345: The model was then tasked with predicting whether each token in the example represented a feature or not, based on the previously generated interpretation.
elements.sent-2411-346: By comparing the model's predictions with ground truth labels, we can assess how accurately the feature interpretation aligns with actual neuron activations.
elements.sent-2411-347: This process helps validate the interpretability of the features learned by SAEs, MLPs, and other models.
elements.sent-2411-348: This pipeline is based on the work of \citet{eleutherOpenSource , which itself builds on the work of others.}
elements.sent-2411-349: This provides a quantification of how interpretable a given neuron or feature actually is.

elements.section-2411-015: Relating Our Amortisation Gap to Prior Results in Sparse Autoencoders
elements.sent-2411-431: In this appendix, we clarify how our amortisation-gap theorem aligns with prior work on shallow autoencoders in the sparse coding literature, including references such as \cite{rangamani2018sparse and \cite{nguyen2019dynamics .}}
elements.sent-2411-432: While these earlier results may appear to contradict our statement that a single feedforward linear-nonlinear encoder cannot globally recover all sparse codes from fewer measurements ($M < N$), we show that these works rely on local or probabilistic assumptions.
elements.sent-2411-433: By contrast, our theorem provides a global, worst-case statement.
elements.sent-2411-434: Our work presents a global impossibility claim: a single-layer linear+\(\sigma\) map cannot perfectly invert every $K$-sparse code if $M<N$.
elements.sent-2411-435: This argument is rank-based and does not rely on training initialisation or a specific data distribution.
elements.sent-2411-436: In contrast, many prior theorems establish local (or near-dictionary) results: they assume the encoder's weights start sufficiently close to the true dictionary, then show that a ReLU (or threshold) gating can maintain or refine correct sparse recovery for typical data.
elements.sent-2411-437: The distinction between uniform and distribution-specific recovery is also important.
elements.sent-2411-438: Our proof deals with uniform, adversarially chosen $K$-sparse codes.
elements.sent-2411-439: If the model must handle all codes in $\mathbb{R ^N$ with $\|s\|_0 \le K$, a single feed-forward pass will inevitably fail for some codes.}
elements.sent-2411-440: By contrast, much of the prior literature -- including \cite{rangamani2018sparse, nguyen2019dynamics --requires that codes are drawn from a random distribution (e.g., sub-Gaussian or mixture-of-Gaussians).}
elements.sent-2411-441: This assumption enables high-probability success on most sampled codes, but does not guarantee recovery of all codes.
elements.sent-2411-442: Another key distinction lies in single-pass versus multi-pass inference.
elements.sent-2411-443: Our amortisation-gap statement explicitly concerns a single-layer feedforward autoencoder.
elements.sent-2411-444: Iterative or unrolled algorithms (e.g., LISTA \citep{gregor2010learning , or multi-layer ReLU stacks) circumvent the rank restriction by repeatedly refining the estimate.}
elements.sent-2411-445: Thus, a multi-iteration or multi-layer approach can approach near-optimal sparse recovery; but this does not contradict our statement about a one-pass linear-nonlinear encoder's inability to decode every sparse signal.
elements.sent-2411-446: Finally, our result demands exact (or perfect) inversion of all feasible codes, while prior analyses often accept approximate or high-probability correctness.
elements.sent-2411-447: They conclude that, given some distribution on codes and an adequately trained near-dictionary encoder, one recovers the support with probability $>1-\delta$.
elements.sent-2411-448: This does not conflict with a global impossibility statement.

elements.sent-2411-459: In this appendix, we explore an alternative data generation process that better reflects the distributional properties observed in real-world latent representations.
elements.sent-2411-460: While our main experiments use uniformly sampled sparse codes, recent work has shown that latent features in large models often follow heavy-tailed distributions (e.g., power laws) with varying activation frequencies \citep{engels2024languagemodelfeatureslinear, park2024geometry .}
elements.sent-2411-461: To investigate the robustness of our findings, we modify our synthetic data generator to incorporate a Zipf distribution (parameterised by $\alpha$) over feature activations.
elements.sent-2411-462: This creates a hierarchical structure where certain features are consistently more likely to be active and have larger magnitudes, while others are more rarely activated.
elements.sent-2411-463: The modified generator maintains the core sparsity constraint of $K$ active dimensions, but weighs both the selection probability and magnitude of each dimension according to its position in the Zipf distribution.
